skill.name: command-development
skill.description: Guidance for designing, documenting, testing, and publishing Claude Code slash commands with frontmatter, arguments, file references, Bash context, and plugin workflows.
skill.summary: Build reliable Claude Code slash commands for repeatable development workflows.
skill.author: anthropics

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, filesystem, network, and cryptography patterns, but review shows they are primarily Markdown documentation and examples for Claude Code slash commands. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found. The skill should publish with a medium warning because it teaches Bash-enabled commands, file references, and tool permissions that can be risky if copied without review.

medium_findings.title: Bash-Enabled Slash Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for capability risk, not malicious intent. The skill documents allowed-tools with Bash access and command output interpolation, which can execute shell commands if users create commands from these examples.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly describe Bash tool access or Bash execution syntax. The context is legitimate documentation, so the confidence is high for capability risk but not for malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: File Reference and Personal Command Path Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for capability risk. The skill explains home-directory command locations and file-reference syntax, which is expected for Claude Code commands but can read sensitive files if command authors use broad paths or untrusted arguments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence directly shows personal command paths and file inclusion behavior. It is a normal feature, but the security impact depends on how generated commands are used.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. Representative flagged areas are Markdown fences, command descriptions, and frontmatter examples, not cryptographic API usage or weak hash implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are documentation examples and do not call cryptographic functions. The scanner likely matched Markdown or abbreviated text patterns out of context.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Windows SAM Static Detection Is False Positive
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged line is a documentation heading for security considerations inside maintenance notes, with no Windows SAM database access or credential extraction behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited context discusses validating inputs and avoiding credentials in command files. No sensitive Windows database path or access pattern appears at the flagged location.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Network URLs in Documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for network activity. The URLs are support and documentation placeholders in marketplace guidance, not executable fetch calls or data exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows example documentation links only. There is no code path that sends data to those URLs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Execution Syntax in Markdown Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation demonstrates Bash execution and shell substitution patterns. These are valid command-development examples but should be reviewed before users copy them into active commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The syntax is plainly present in documentation. The risk is contextual because Markdown itself is not executed by installing the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Paths and Temporary Workspace Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples reference personal command directories, debug logs, file inclusion, and temporary workspaces. These are normal for command development, but they can expose local data if used carelessly.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples clearly involve local paths. They are instructional and not malicious, so confidence is moderate-high for risk pattern only.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded Example URLs
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes example support and documentation URLs. They are not active network calls, but publishers should replace placeholders before release.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The evidence supports only documentation placeholder usage. It remains useful to flag because stale or incorrect support links can mislead users.

content.value_statement: Teams need repeatable AI workflows that are easy to run and maintain. This skill helps create Claude Code slash commands with clear structure, safe tool limits, and practical examples.
actual_capabilities: Explains Claude Code slash command file structure and command locations.
Documents YAML frontmatter fields such as description, allowed-tools, model, and argument hints.
Shows how to use dynamic arguments, positional parameters, and file references.
Provides patterns for Bash-backed context and limited tool permissions.
Covers plugin command organization, marketplace guidance, and testing strategies.
Includes examples for review, testing, deployment, documentation, and interactive workflows.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not create command files automatically.
Generated command safety still depends on the user selected tools and arguments.
Bash examples require careful review before use in shared repositories.
Some examples use placeholder URLs and paths that must be replaced before publishing.

use_cases.title: Create Team Review Commands
use_cases.description: Standardize code review, security review, and pull request workflows with reusable project commands.

use_cases.title: Package Plugin Workflows
use_cases.description: Design plugin commands that use clear names, scoped tools, arguments, and supporting documentation.

use_cases.title: Test Command Behavior
use_cases.description: Validate command arguments, file references, interactive prompts, and failure handling before release.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Claude Code slash command for reviewing a pull request. Include a short description, expected arguments, and the exact command prompt text.

prompt_templates.title: Add Frontmatter and Tools
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a slash command that can read files and run only git status commands. Explain the frontmatter choices and any safety limits.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Interactive Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an interactive command that asks the user to choose a target environment before preparing a deployment checklist.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Plugin Command Set
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this set of plugin slash commands for naming, argument design, allowed tools, documentation quality, and marketplace readiness.

output_examples.input: Create a command that reviews changed files before a pull request.
output_examples.output: A concise command specification with a name, help description, allowed tools, argument guidance, and review instructions.

output_examples.input: Improve this deployment command for safer production use.
output_examples.output: A revised workflow that checks branch state, asks for confirmation, limits shell access, and stops on failed validation.

output_examples.input: Prepare marketplace notes for my plugin commands.
output_examples.output: A publication checklist covering command names, descriptions, examples, permissions, limitations, and support information.

best_practices: Keep allowed tools narrow and match them to the command purpose.
Validate arguments and file references before using them in command workflows.
Document expected inputs, outputs, failure cases, and safe operating limits.
anti_patterns: Do not grant all tools when a command only needs read or git access.
Do not pass untrusted arguments directly into shell commands.
Do not publish commands with placeholder paths, URLs, or support contacts.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps you design Claude Code slash commands for repeated development, review, testing, deployment, and plugin workflows.

faq.question: Does it run commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation and examples. Users must create and run command files in their own environment.

faq.question: Can it help with command frontmatter?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains fields such as description, allowed-tools, model, argument-hint, and related usage patterns.

faq.question: Does it cover plugin commands?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes plugin command locations, naming, component integration, marketplace considerations, and testing advice.

faq.question: What is the main safety concern?
faq.answer: Commands can grant file or Bash access. Review tool permissions and argument handling before sharing commands.

faq.question: Can Codex users use this content?
faq.answer: Yes. Codex users can use the guidance to draft, review, and document Claude Code command workflows.
